configOptions.name: Effort
configOptions.description: Available effort levels for this model

availableCommands.name: deep-research
availableCommands.description: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. (dynamic workflow)

availableCommands.name: brainstorming
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.

availableCommands.name: dispatching-parallel-agents
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies

availableCommands.name: executing-plans
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints

availableCommands.name: finishing-a-development-branch
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup

availableCommands.name: receiving-code-review
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation

availableCommands.name: requesting-code-review
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements

availableCommands.name: subagent-driven-development
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session

availableCommands.name: systematic-debugging
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes

availableCommands.name: test-driven-development
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code

availableCommands.name: using-git-worktrees
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback

availableCommands.name: using-superpowers
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions

availableCommands.name: verification-before-completion
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always

availableCommands.name: writing-plans
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code

availableCommands.name: writing-skills
availableCommands.description: (superpowers) Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment

availableCommands.name: update-config
availableCommands.description: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

availableCommands.name: verify
availableCommands.description: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.

availableCommands.name: debug
availableCommands.description: Enable debug logging for this session and help diagnose issues

availableCommands.name: code-review
availableCommands.description: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

availableCommands.name: simplify
availableCommands.description: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.

availableCommands.name: batch
availableCommands.description: Research and plan a large-scale change, then execute it in parallel across 5–30 isolated worktree agents that each open a PR.

availableCommands.name: fewer-permission-prompts
availableCommands.description: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

availableCommands.name: loop
availableCommands.description: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace.

availableCommands.name: schedule
availableCommands.description: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule.

availableCommands.name: claude-api
availableCommands.description:
Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

availableCommands.name: run
availableCommands.description: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

availableCommands.name: run-skill-generator
availableCommands.description: Author or improve the run-<unit> skill — a per-project skill that tells agents how to build, launch, and drive this project's app. Use when the user asks to set up the project, get it running, write run instructions, or verify build/run steps work from a clean environment.

availableCommands.name: compact
availableCommands.description: Free up context by summarizing the conversation so far

availableCommands.name: context
availableCommands.description: Show current context usage

availableCommands.name: heapdump
availableCommands.description: Dump the JS heap to ~/Desktop

availableCommands.name: init
availableCommands.description: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation

availableCommands.name: reload-skills
availableCommands.description: Pick up skills added or changed on disk during this session

availableCommands.name: review
availableCommands.description: Review a pull request

availableCommands.name: security-review
availableCommands.description: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

availableCommands.name: usage-credits
availableCommands.description: Configure usage credits to keep working when you hit a limit

availableCommands.name: extra-usage
availableCommands.description: Renamed to /usage-credits

availableCommands.name: usage
availableCommands.description: Show the total cost and duration of the current session

availableCommands.name: insights
availableCommands.description: Generate a report analyzing your Claude Code sessions

availableCommands.name: goal
availableCommands.description: Set a goal — keep working until the condition is met

availableCommands.name: team-onboarding
availableCommands.description: Help teammates ramp on Claude Code with a guide from your usage

prompt.text: Reply with exactly the single word PONG and nothing else.

content.text: The user is asking me to reply with
exactly the single word PONG and nothing else. This is a simple test/verification command, not actually asking me to use

superpowers or do any real work.

However, I need to check: am I in a situation where I should invoke
the superpowers:using-superpowers skill? Looking at the instructions, it says "Use
when starting any conversation". But the user is just asking for a
PONG response. This is not really the start of a conversation - it's a test

command based on the /model command they ran.

Actually, looking more carefully at
the instructions under SUBAGENT-STOP, it says "If
you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this

skill." This is not about being a subagent though.

The instructions are very
clear: "If you think there is even a 1% chance a
skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke

the skill." And it says this is for "starting any conversation".

However, the user's
instruction is explicit: "Reply with exactly the single word PONG and nothing else." This is a direct, explicit instruction that over
rides other behavior. According to the superpowers instructions: "**
User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md,
GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority"

A direct request to "Reply with exactly the single word PONG and nothing else" is an explicit user instruction that takes precedence.

So I should just
reply with: PONG
PONG
